section.title: Hezekiah's Illness and Recovery
section.sectionRationale: Within the Hezekiah narrative interlude, chapters 36 to 39, this chapter turns from public deliverance from Assyria to a private crisis: the king's own mortal illness. Hezekiah's tearful prayer, the LORD's gracious answer of fifteen added years, and his own written song of near death and rescued life prepare the reader for the far larger crisis of exile that the next two chapters begin to unfold.
section.thesis: Facing a death sentence from the LORD's own mouth, Hezekiah weeps and prays rather than simply resigning himself to it, and the LORD who is sovereign even over the sun's shadow graciously adds fifteen years to his life, teaching that prayer reaches the ear of God even when His will has already been announced.
section.frameNote: Six movements: Hezekiah's mortal illness announced; his prayer and bitter weeping; the LORD's answer of fifteen years and a sign in the sun's shadow; Hezekiah's own written song of descent toward Sheol; his song of thanksgiving for restored life; and a closing note on the means of healing and the sign requested.

verses.text: In those days Hezekiah became mortally ill. The prophet Isaiah son of Amoz came to him and said, “This is what the LORD says: ‘Put your house in order, for you are about to die; you will not recover.’”

units.label: Hezekiah's prayer and tears
units.note: remember how I have walked before You

verses.text: Then Hezekiah turned his face to the wall and prayed to the LORD,
saying, “Please, O LORD, remember how I have walked before You faithfully and with wholehearted devotion; I have done what was good in Your sight.” And Hezekiah wept bitterly.

units.label: The LORD's answer: fifteen years and a sign
units.note: the shadow goes back ten steps

verses.text: And the word of the LORD came to Isaiah, saying,
“Go and tell Hezekiah that this is what the LORD, the God of your father David, says: ‘I have heard your prayer; I have seen your tears. Behold, I will add fifteen years to your life.
And I will deliver you and this city from the hand of the king of Assyria. I will defend this city.
This will be a sign to you from the LORD that He will do what He has promised:
I will make the sun's shadow that falls on the stairway of Ahaz go back ten steps.’” So the sunlight went back the ten steps it had descended.

units.label: Hezekiah's song: descent toward Sheol
units.note: I must go through the gates of the dead

verses.text: This is a writing by Hezekiah king of Judah after his illness and recovery:
I said, “In the prime of my life I must go through the gates of {{sheol|Sheol}} and be deprived of the remainder of my years.”
I said, “I will never again see the LORD, even the LORD, in the land of the living; I will no longer look on mankind with those who dwell in this world.
My dwelling has been picked up and removed from me like a shepherd's tent. I have rolled up my life like a weaver; He cuts me off from the loom; from day until night You make an end of me.
I composed myself until the morning. Like a lion He breaks all my bones; from day until night You make an end of me.
I chirp like a swallow or crane; I moan like a dove. My eyes grow weak as I look upward. O Lord, I am oppressed; be my security.”

units.label: Hezekiah's song: thanksgiving for restored life
units.note: You have cast all my sins behind Your back

verses.text: What can I say? He has spoken to me, and He Himself has done this. I will walk slowly all my years because of the anguish of my soul.
O Lord, by such things men live, and in all of them my spirit finds life. You have restored me to health and have let me live.
Surely for my own welfare I had such great anguish; but Your love has delivered me from the pit of oblivion, for You have cast all my sins behind Your back.
For Sheol cannot thank You; Death cannot praise You. Those who descend to the Pit cannot hope for Your faithfulness.
The {{chai|living}}, only the living, can thank You, as I do today; fathers will tell their children about Your faithfulness.
The LORD will save me; we will play songs on stringed instruments all the days of our lives in the house of the LORD.
Now Isaiah had said, “Prepare a lump of pressed figs and apply it to the boil, and he will recover.”
And Hezekiah had asked, “What will be the sign that I will go up to the house of the LORD?”

sheol.gloss: The realm of the dead, the shadowy world beneath, not yet the fuller revelation of heaven and hell later Scripture unfolds. Hezekiah dreads its gates because there, he says, the dead cannot praise God among the living, which is exactly why his rescue from its brink becomes an occasion for public thanksgiving rather than private relief alone.

chai.gloss: Living, alive, from the root chayah, to live. Hezekiah insists that only the living can thank God and tell the next generation of His faithfulness, so his added years are not merely personal gain but a fresh opportunity to testify, a theme that runs through Scripture's whole understanding of why God grants life and health.

basic.context: Hezekiah receives the hardest possible word from Isaiah: he is dying, and he will not recover. Rather than accepting this in silence, he turns to the wall and weeps bitterly in prayer, reminding God of his faithful life. The LORD hears him, adds fifteen years, confirms it with an extraordinary sign in the sun's shadow, and Hezekiah later writes his own poem describing both his terror at death's approach and his gratitude at being rescued.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real historical episode, also told in 2 Kings 20 and 2 Chronicles 32, placed here after the Assyrian deliverance to prepare for the Babylonian envoys of chapter 39 and the exile that the Book of Comfort, beginning in chapter 40, will address. It shows that a word from God announcing His will is not necessarily unconditional or unchangeable when met with humble, faithful prayer, without suggesting that every prayer for healing will be answered so visibly.
basic.originalLanguages: Hezekiah dreads passing through the gates of {{sheol}}, the realm of the dead, where he says the dead cannot praise God. His whole argument for wanting to live rests on being among the {{chai}}, the living, who alone can thank the LORD and teach the next generation His faithfulness, a reason for longing to live that reaches beyond mere self-preservation.
basic.typology: Hezekiah's descent in thought to the gates of death and his rescue back to the land of the living, followed immediately by public thanksgiving, anticipates the pattern later fulfilled fully in Christ, who actually entered death and was raised to life never to die again, securing life for His people beyond anything a mere extension of years could offer.
basic.christ: Hezekiah's prayer with bitter weeping over a sentence of death, and God's compassionate answer, echoes forward to Christ, who 'offered up prayers and petitions with loud cries and tears to the One who could save Him from death,' Hebrews 5:7. Hezekiah's testimony that the LORD 'cast all my sins behind Your back,' verse 17, anticipates the far greater and final casting away of sin accomplished at the cross, where Christ's own death, not a sundial's reversed shadow, is the decisive sign that God keeps His word.
basic.god: God is sovereign even over the sun's shadow, able to give a sign that reverses the ordinary course of a day, yet He is also the God who hears the tears of one man and adjusts the very length of his life in response to prayer. He is not moved by cold necessity but genuinely responsive to those who bring their fear and grief honestly before Him.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Kings 20:1-11 and 2 Chronicles 32:24-26 narrate the same illness and healing. Psalm 30 is a similar song of thanksgiving after being rescued from death. Micah 7:19 uses the same image of casting sins away, into the depths of the sea. Hebrews 5:7 describes Christ's own prayers with loud cries and tears in the face of death.
basic.oneStory: Hezekiah's brush with death and his rescued, thankful life is a small window into the whole Bible's pattern: God hears the cries of those who fear death, and grants life not merely for its own sake but so His faithfulness can be told to the next generation. That pattern reaches its full and final form in Christ, who conquered death itself so that His people might live and praise God forever.

context.literary: A narrative frame, verses 1 to 8 and 21 to 22, surrounding a personal poem, verses 9 to 20, that moves from terrified lament over approaching death to grateful thanksgiving for rescued life, a structure similar to many individual psalms of lament that turn to praise.
context.historical: Set in the same general period as the Assyrian crisis of chapters 36 to 37, likely shortly before or around it, since the LORD's promise to add fifteen years and defend the city from Assyria, verse 6, ties this illness closely to that larger deliverance.
context.canonical: Part of the Hezekiah interlude, chapters 36 to 39, and paralleled closely by 2 Kings 20:1-11 and 2 Chronicles 32:24-26; its placement after the Assyrian deliverance and before the Babylonian envoys of chapter 39 prepares readers for the exile that the Book of Comfort will address from chapter 40 onward.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that even a word from God announcing His settled will can be met with humble, faithful, tearful prayer, and that the LORD who governs the sun's shadow itself hears such prayer and graciously grants what was asked.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical answer to prayer, including a unique confirming sign; it is not a promise that every illness will be reversed or that every prayer will receive an extension of years. Its abiding principle is that God hears sincere prayer and that life is given, when it is given, for the purpose of praising Him and passing on His faithfulness.

debates.question: Why does Isaiah place this illness after the Assyrian deliverance in chapters 36 to 37, when 2 Kings 20 suggests it occurred before or during that crisis?

views.label: Isaiah arranges chapters theologically, not strictly chronologically
views.case: Isaiah's placement builds from public deliverance from Assyria, to private crisis and healing, to the Babylonian envoys of chapter 39, forming a deliberate theological progression toward the exile and comfort that follow, regardless of the precise historical sequence.

views.label: The events may be roughly contemporaneous
views.case: The LORD's promise in verse 6 to defend the city from Assyria ties this illness closely in time to that crisis, so the ordering may reflect events that occurred close together without strict chronological precision being the author's concern.

debates.bearing: Either way, the placement serves Isaiah's larger theological purpose: showing a faithful king rescued from death just before Judah faces the far greater national crisis of coming exile.

study.originalLanguages: Hezekiah's fear centers on the gates of {{sheol}}, the shadowy realm of the dead, where, he argues, the dead cannot praise God as the {{chai}}, the living, still can. His whole plea for life is bound up with wanting to keep testifying to God's faithfulness among the living, not mere self-preservation.

typology.type: Hezekiah's rescue from the gates of death followed by public thanksgiving
typology.fulfillment: Christ's actual death and resurrection, securing life beyond any mere extension of years, never to die again.

typology.type: The LORD casting Hezekiah's sins behind His back
typology.fulfillment: The complete removal of sin's guilt accomplished at the cross, Micah 7:19, 1 John 1:9.

study.christ: Hezekiah's tearful prayer in the face of a death sentence, and God's compassionate response, anticipates Christ, who 'offered up prayers and petitions with loud cries and tears to the One who could save Him from death,' Hebrews 5:7, though Christ's path led through death itself to resurrection rather than around it. Hezekiah's confession that the LORD 'cast all my sins behind Your back,' verse 17, is a foretaste of the far greater and final removal of sin secured at the cross, where God's faithfulness to His word is proven not by a sundial but by an empty tomb.
study.god: God governs even the sun's shadow, able to give an extraordinary sign that reverses the ordinary order of a day, yet He is near enough to hear one man's bitter weeping and to respond by adding years to his life. He is neither distant nor bound by fatalism; He genuinely responds to honest, faithful prayer.

crossReferences.note: The parallel account of the same illness, prayer, and healing, including further detail about the sign of the sun's shadow.
The Chronicler's briefer account, noting Hezekiah's pride after his healing and his subsequent humbling.
A similar song of thanksgiving for being rescued from the brink of death, echoing the movement of Hezekiah's own poem.
Describes Christ's own prayers with loud cries and tears in the face of death, echoing Hezekiah's tearful prayer here.

study.oneStory: Hezekiah's brush with death, his honest tears, and his rescued, thankful life form a small window into the whole Bible's larger pattern: a God who hears the cries of those who fear death and grants life so that His faithfulness can be told to the next generation. That pattern finds its full and final form in Christ, who conquered death itself so His people could live and praise God forever.

quiz.q: What word does Isaiah first bring to Hezekiah in verse 1?

options.text: That he is mortally ill and will not recover; he should put his house in order.
options.note: Verse 1 delivers exactly this sobering word from the LORD through Isaiah.

options.text: That his enemies will soon attack the city.
options.note: The word concerns Hezekiah's own health, not an external military threat.

options.text: That he will be given great wealth.
options.note: The word is a sentence of death, not a promise of wealth.

options.text: That his reign will be extended by fifty years immediately.
options.note: The initial word is a death sentence; the extension of years comes only after Hezekiah's prayer.

quiz.q: How does Hezekiah respond to this news in verses 2 to 3?

options.text: He turns to the wall, prays, and weeps bitterly, reminding the LORD of his faithful life.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3 describe exactly this tearful, honest prayer.

options.text: He accepts the news calmly with no prayer at all.
options.note: The opposite; he responds with fervent, tearful prayer, not passive acceptance.

options.text: He immediately summons foreign physicians instead of praying.
options.note: His first response recorded is prayer, not seeking foreign medical help.

options.text: He blames Isaiah for delivering the message.
options.note: He directs his response to the LORD in prayer, not blame toward the prophet.

quiz.q: What sign does the LORD give to confirm His promise in verses 7 to 8?

options.text: The sun's shadow on the stairway of Ahaz goes back ten steps.
options.note: Verse 8 describes this extraordinary sign exactly.

options.text: A sudden earthquake shakes Jerusalem.
options.note: The specific sign given is the reversal of the sun's shadow, not an earthquake.

options.text: A rainbow appears over the temple.
options.note: Not the sign described in this chapter; the sign concerns the sun's shadow.

options.text: A dream given to Hezekiah's son.
options.note: The sign is a visible, public reversal of the sun's shadow, not a private dream.

quiz.q: What reason does Hezekiah give in verse 19 for wanting to keep living?

options.text: Only the living can thank God, and fathers can tell their children about His faithfulness.
options.note: Verse 19 gives exactly this reason, connecting life to ongoing praise and testimony.

options.text: He wants to accumulate more wealth and possessions.
options.note: His stated reason concerns praise and testimony, not material gain.

options.text: He wants to avoid all future hardship entirely.
options.note: His reasoning centers on praising God and teaching the next generation, not merely avoiding hardship.

options.text: He wants to outlive his political rivals.
options.note: Not his stated reason; the text gives a reason rooted in worship and testimony, not political rivalry.

quiz.q: What does Hezekiah say the LORD has done with his sins in verse 17?

options.text: Cast all his sins behind His back.
options.note: Verse 17 states this directly, a vivid image of complete forgiveness.

options.text: Recorded them permanently for future judgment.
options.note: The opposite; the image is one of sins being removed from view, not preserved for judgment.

options.text: Forgiven only some of his sins.
options.note: The image of casting sins behind God's back conveys complete removal, not partial forgiveness.

options.text: Transferred them to his descendants.
options.note: Not the meaning of this verse, which describes God removing Hezekiah's own sins from view.

questions: Hezekiah wept bitterly and reminded God of his faithful life rather than accepting the news of death in silence. How honest are you with God when you receive news you cannot control?
Hezekiah's reason for wanting more years was to keep praising God and telling the next generation of His faithfulness. What would it look like for you to live your added days with that same purpose?
The LORD confirmed His word with a sign that reversed the ordinary course of the sun. Where do you need fresh confidence that God's promises to you are trustworthy, even without a visible sign?
Hezekiah rejoiced that his sins were cast behind God's back. Is there a sin you are still carrying that Christ has already fully removed at the cross?
Hezekiah's brush with death led to public thanksgiving in the house of the LORD. What has God rescued you from that you have not yet turned into open thanksgiving?

translationNotes.note: The stairway of Ahaz was likely a sundial-like structure whose steps marked the sun's shadow through the day; the sign reversed the shadow's ordinary movement.
This poem is presented as Hezekiah's own composition, a rare instance of a king's personal psalm preserved within a prophetic book.
These closing verses supply narrative details, the fig poultice and Hezekiah's request for a sign, that read almost as an appendix to the main account, paralleling similar detail in 2 Kings 20:7-8.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 38:1-22. No em or en dashes appeared in the source text for this passage.
